sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Michelle, a 4-year-old mother, presented with a knee injury sustained while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She fell and heard a pop in her knee, which has been painful ever since.
She was unable to bear weight and was taken down by the ski patrol.
She reports pain on the outside of her knee and a feeling of instability, as if the knee is going to buckle.
She has been experiencing decreased flexion and extension, and pain with palpation of the lateral knee.
She has been taking ibuprofen for pain, which has provided some relief.
Past medical history: The patient reports being otherwise generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other medical conditions.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness or tingling in her toes and reports being able to feel her toes.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, a palpable dorsalis pedis pulse, and a palpable posterior tibial pulse.
There is no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee, which requires further evaluation with an MRI to confirm.
Problem list: ACL rupture; right knee pain; right knee instability; decreased flexion and extension.
Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the suspected ACL rupture.
I recommended that the patient continue taking ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
I also advised her to use a knee brace to provide support.
I will follow up with her to discuss the results of the MRI and determine the next steps in management.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again soon to discuss the results of the MRI and develop a plan for treatment.
